paper_title: Probing Cultural Signals in Large Language Models through Author Profiling

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in applications with societal impact, raising concerns about the cultural biases they encode. We probe these representations by evaluating whether LLMs can perform author profiling from song lyrics in a zero-shot setting, inferring singers' gender and ethnicity without task-specific fine-tuning. Across several open-source models evaluated on more than 10,000 lyrics, we find that LLMs achieve non-trivial profiling performance but demonstrate systematic cultural alignment: most models default toward North American ethnicity, while DeepSeek-1.5B aligns more strongly with Asian ethnicity. This finding emerges from both the models' prediction distributions and an analysis of their generated rationales. To quantify these disparities, we introduce two fairness metrics, Modality Accuracy Divergence (MAD) and Recall Divergence (RD), and show that Ministral-8B displays the strongest ethnicity bias among the evaluated models, whereas Gemma-12B shows the most balanced behavior. Our code is available on [GitHub](https://github.com/ValentinLafargue/CulturalProbingLLM) and results on [HuggingFace](https://huggingface.co/datasets/ValentinLAFARGUE/AuthorProfilingResults).

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in applications with societal impact, raising concerns about the cultural biases they encode. We probe these representations by evaluating whether LLMs can perform author profiling from song lyrics in zero-shot setting, inferring singers gender and ethnicity without task-specific fine-tuning. Across several open-source models evaluated on more than 10,000 lyrics, we find that LLMs achieve non-trivial profiling performance but demonstrate systematic cultural alignment: most models default toward North American ethnicity, while DeepSeek-1.5B aligns more strongly with Asian ethnicity. This finding emerges from both the models prediction distributions and an analysis of their generated rationales. To quantify these disparities, we introduce two fairness metrics, Modality Accuracy Divergence (MAD) and Recall Divergence (RD), and show that Ministral-8B displays the strongest ethnicity bias among the evaluated models, whereas Gemma-12B shows the most balanced behavior. Our code is available on GitHub and results on HuggingFace.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly embedded in socially consequential domains such as education (Chiang et al., 2024; Vie and Kashima, 2019; Nebhi et al., 2025) and content moderation (Mullick et al., 2023; Yadav et al., 2025; Gligoric et al., 2024). In educational settings, LLM-based tutoring and essay feedback systems may misinterpret writing diversity as lower quality, thereby reinforcing academic inequities (Weissburg et al., 2025). In content moderation, models have been shown to disproportionately flag dialectal expressions, risking the silencing of minority voices (Davidson et al., 2019; Sap et al., 2019). These harms might not arise from failures of factual knowledge as evaluated in (Wu et al., 2025; Qiu 1 et al., 2025), but from failures to correctly interpret and represent cultural identity (Tao et al., 2024). To evaluate the potential skewness of the cultural representation embedded in LLMs, we make the models do author profiling. It is the task of inferring sociodemographic and psychological attributes of the author from their creative output (Ouni et al., 2023). For written creations, the task relies on cultural specific references but also on linguistic patterns present in an authors writing, with applications across multiple domains (Argamon et al., 2009; Lanza-Cruz et al., 2023; Saxena et al., 2025; Wickramasekara et al., 2025). In this work, we examine the extent to which LLMs can infer the gender and the ethnicity of singers, based solely on song lyrics. Song lyrics constitute rich yet understudied domain for author profiling. They combine personal expression, genre-specific conventions, and culturally embedded linguistic markers (Hu and Downie, 2010; DeWall et al., 2011). Compared to shorter and more curated texts, such as social media posts, lyrics are often less censored, stylistically diverse, and more deeply rooted in cultural contexts (Espejo, 2008; Eder, 2013; Ellis et al., 2015). Studying the LLMs cultural representation through song lyrics is not about music per se, but about evaluating how the LLMs understand and evaluate the high-density normative environments present in them. In sociology, ethnicity is understood as socially constructed cultural identity, maintained through shared practices, narratives, and symbolic boundaries rather than biological ancestry or nationality (Weber, 1922; Barth, 1969; Hall and du Gay, 1996). Ethnicity is thus expressed and mediated through language, style, and discourse, making it amenable to textual analysis (Malmasi et al., 2017; PreotiucPietro and Ungar, 2018). In this work, we adopt this sociological perspective and treat ethnicity as perceived ethno-cultural identity as reflected in linguistic cues, rather than as factual property such as place of birth, race, or genetic origin. Similarly, we emphasize that we consider the socially constructed gender and not the sex of the individual. The LLMs we evaluate are not fine-tuned for demographic classification (Wickramasekara et al., 2025), but are instruction-tuned next-token predictors (Ouyang et al., 2022). Prior work has shown that LLMs can perform zero-shot reasoning (Kojima et al., 2022) and authorship-related inference without fine-tuning (Huang et al., 2024). LLMs encode extensive linguistic and cultural knowledge but also inherit representational biases from the data on which they are trained (AlKhamissi et al., 2024; Schramowski et al., 2021; Caliskan et al., 2017). From cognitive sociology roots (Friedman, 2019), we will call cultural blindness when LLM does not recognize cultural clue, or when it ignores it. For instance, we consider the following example from DeepSeek-1.5B ethnicity reasoning on Miriam Makebas Piece Of Ground: The context of the discovery of gold and the transatlantic slave trade aligns with African American history, suggesting narrative from Asia. We address the following research questions: (i) to what extent can LLMs perform author profiling on song lyrics in zero-shot setting ; (ii) which factors influence the profiling decision and performance ; (iii) and whether their predictions exhibit systematic biases across gender and ethnic categories. By evaluating multiple LLMs on curated dataset of song lyrics, we show that these models systematically mispredict certain gender and ethnic categories, revealing model-specific cultural alignments. Our analysis suggests that some LLMs rely disproportionately on the dominant ethno-cultural norms, and that their representations of ethnicity reflect uneven sensitivity to less-represented cultural groups. Our contributions are threefold: We evaluate the ability of LLMs to perform author profiling on song lyrics without finetuning, using sociolinguistically informed prompts, and show that most models achieve non-trivial performance. We evaluate the biases of the LLMs through statistical tests analysing modality-based distribution disparities and thought the fairness metrics we introduce, MAD and RD. We show that instruction-style prompting elicits high-quality rationales from LLMs, producing interpretable explanations that are useful for prompt design and for analysing cultural representations encoded in language models.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Author profiling refers to the task of inferring sociodemographic attributes of an author from their creative output. Early work by Argamon et al. framed author profiling as text classification problem, showing that both content-based and stylistic features vary systematically with attributes such as gender and language use (Argamon et al., 2009). Subsequent research, including the PAN shared tasks organized by the Webis group1, has established standard benchmarks and datasets for profiling attributes such as gender and age, primarily using supervised classifiers and explicit feature engineering (HaCohen-Kerner et al., 2018; Morales Sánchez et al., 2022). large body of sociolinguistic research has documented systematic differences in language use across genders. Foundational studies have identified gendered patterns in politeness strategies, emotional expression, modality, and lexical choice (Lakoff, 1973; Holmes, 1988; Mulac and Lundell, 1994; Schwartz et al., 2013; Schler et al., 2006). These findings provide empirical grounding for inferring gender from text and motivate the use of linguistic cues as signals of social identity. Beyond gender, prior work has explored the inference of ethnicity from language use, particularly in social media contexts. Preotiuc-Pietro et al. demonstrated that linguistic patterns are associated with census-based racial and ethnic categories, highlighting the role of language as an expression of socially constructed ethnic identity (PreotiucPietro and Ungar, 2018). While some associations, such as links between African-American authorship and toxicity, have been contested (Andrade et al., 2024), this line of work underscores that ethnicity is reflected in textual practices. More recently, large language models (LLMs) have been investigated for author profiling and related authorship tasks. Studies have shown that LLMs can predict demographic attributes such as age and gender when fine-tuned on task-specific corpora, often outperforming classical models but also exhibiting limitations related to training data 1https://pan.webis.de biases (Cho et al., 2024). Other work has demonstrated that pre-trained language models can perform zero-shot authorship attribution and verification, capturing stylistic regularities without supervised labels (Rivera-Soto et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2024). However, these studies have largely focused on authorship attribution or classification accuracy, with limited attention to sociodemographic inference or cultural bias. In contrast to prior work, we study author profiling in zero-shot setting using LLMs on song lyrics, long-form and culturally rich domain that has received little attention in profiling research. We analyze not only prediction accuracy but also systematic biases across gender and ethnicity, and we examine how prompt design and model explanations relate to established sociolinguistic cues. Rather than explicitly modeling linguistic features, we use them as an analytical lens to interpret LLMgenerated predictions and rationales.

sections.title: 3.1 Data source
sections.content: We have obtained song lyrics from both Deezer and Spotify. To obtain Deezer song lyrics, we used the Wasabi dataset for metadata combined with Genius lyrics using their API 2. For Spotify song lyrics, we obtained the lyrics from the Spotify dataset and used the MusicBrainz API 3 to obtain artist metadata.

sections.title: 3.2 Filtering process
sections.content: A potential challenge is the presence of co-writers or ghostwriters. To mitigate this issue, we exclude bands from our dataset and focus on solo artists, assuming that artists select songs that align with their public artistic identity even when they are not the sole lyricists. Under this assumption, inferring the perceived ethnicity of the lyricist remains informative for analyzing cultural representation in model predictions. We construct our corpus by merging two lyric datasets and harmonizing their metadata. We similarly normalize artist gender labels (e.g., man, woman, group, band, non-binary, other) into unified schema, and, for the purposes of statistical analysis and model comparison, we retain only songs by solo artists annotated as either man or woman, filtering out groups, non-binary, and ambiguous 2https://genius.com/ 3https://musicbrainz.org/ cases. We had to remove the non-binary singers as we did not have enough individuals to have statistically significant results (5 artists with less than 10 songs each). We acknowledge the lack of transgender representation in our dataset, which reflects limitations of available metadata and constitutes an important direction for future work. We standardize the artists region by mapping the raw country and ethnicity tags onto six macro-regions (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, Oceania, and South America). We obtain after filtering 10808 songs (7315 from Spotify and 3493 from Deezer) and 2973 unique artists. Later in the results, we mostly consider two subsamples of our dataset to have an ethnicity-balanced dataset and use this one to create gender-balanced dataset. 3.3 Translation We translated all non-English lyrics to English prior to performing the author profiling task. While translation may introduce its own artifacts, this choice allows us to control for overt grammatical gender cues and focus on stylistic and semantic signals, which is central to our research question. We performed all translations using Mistral Small 3.2 in zero-shot configuration, with the translation prompt and more details provided in Appendix H.2.

sections.title: 4.1 Model Selection
sections.content: To evaluate the ability of LLMs to do author profiling and to investigate their cultural representation, we selected diverse set of small-to-medium opensource language models ranging from 7B to 24B parameters (with one smaller model). Prior work finds that LLM memorization capacity tends to increase with model size (Carlini et al., 2023); hence, restricting to 724B is pragmatic step to reduce memorization risk compared to much larger models while still enabling strong evaluation. Their training sample being equivalently smaller, the models are less likely to have seen the non-translated songs in their training data. Our qualitative analysis later shows that the model tested did not recognize lyrics from renowned singers such as Miriam Makeba (active year 19532008) or Eminem (active year 1988present) in the Appendix, Sec. A. We hypothesize that differences in training data composition and curation practices, which is often correlated with the geographic and institutional context of model development, may introduce sys3 Figure 1: End-to-end workflow : lyric collection and metadata harmonization, filtering and balancing by artist gender and macro-region (ethnicity), translation of non-English lyrics into English, zero-shot author-profiling with multiple instruction-tuned LLMs under several prompt variants, and bias/performance analysis, complemented by self-explanation auditing. tematic biases. To test this hypothesis, we selected models from three distinct geographic regions: two models developed by Chinese companies (Qwen 2.5 7B (and An Yang et al., 2024) from Alibaba Cloud and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B (Guo et al., 2025) from DeepSeek), two models from American companies (Llama-3.1-8B (Kassianik et al., 2025) from Meta and Gemma-3-12B (Kamath et al., 2025) from Google), and two models from European companies (Ministral 8B (Liu et al., 2026) and Mistral Small 3.2 24B (Jiang et al., 2023), both from Mistral AI). All selected models have demonstrated competitive performance on instruction-following and reasoning tasks, ensuring that part of observed failures in demographic inference are more likely attributable to bias rather than general model incompetence.

sections.title: 4.2 Prompts
sections.content: We design five prompts (see Sec. L), organized as an incremental sequence where each new prompt extends the preceding prompt by introducing an additional instruction or constraint. 1. Regular prompt: directly asking the model to infer the sociodemographic criteria. 2. Informed prompt: We specify the following sentence to the model: Use lyrical content, tone, perspective, cultural references, and language patterns to decide. 4. Well-informed and expressive: We additionally ask the model to evaluate socio-linguistic attributes such as politeness or confidence. We consider two variants of the prompt: one with the attributes evaluation first and then sociodemographic inference, the second starts with the sociodemographic inference and then evaluate the socio-linguistic attributes. 5. Corrected informed prompt: Using rationales results from the previous prompt results, we inform the model to avoid making consistent specific errors for the ethnicity prediction. More precisely, we add to the informed prompt an additional sentence clarifying that to predict ethnicity, the model should not take into account the theme nor the emotions.

sections.title: 5 Bias evaluation methodology
sections.content: The LLMs we use for author profiling are not finetuned for this task. Hence not only the overall profiling performance should be studied, but also performance stratified by ethnicity, which corresponds to the models ability to profile authors within gender and ethnic group. We point out that most of the works in author profiling either do not consider such fairness-related issues (i.e., see PAN competition papers introduced above) or they consider setting where the text on which the author profiling is done is chosen to be non-informative, as in (Panda et al., 2025). 3. Informed and expressive prompt: We further ask for keywords and explanations from the LLM, for both gender and ethnicity.

sections.title: 5.1 Statistical Tests
sections.content: We evaluate whether LLMs preserve the distribution of sociodemographic modalities in balanced 4 dataset by comparing the inferred and ground-truth attribute distributions. Under the null hypothesis H0 that the model represents all modalities equally, these distributions should match. We test this hypothesis using three complementary measures: chi-squared test, Central Limit Theorembased test, and Wasserstein distancebased test, following prior work methodology (Lafargue et al., 2025). To account for sampling variability, we apply stratified bootstrap resampling (1,000 iterations), drawing 300 songs per ethnicity modality and 500 per gender modality, and report the result for 95% confidence level using p-values. This approach has however notable limitations. It disregards the models errors and it inherently binary, in that hypothesis is either rejected or it is not, making it impossible to quantify the magnitude of the biases detected. To remedy to both limitations, we can also investigate the use of fairness criteria.

sections.title: 5.2 Modality Accuracy Divergence
sections.content: Regular fairness metrics such as the Disparate Impact evaluate whether the prediction (model outcome) is independent of the sensitive attribute (i.e., ethnicity). Yet here, the outcome of the model is the prediction of the sensitive attribute itself. We therefore introduce fairness metric called Modality Accuracy Divergence (MAD). MAD measures how uneven the models accuracy is across the categories of given sensitive attribute. It quantifies the extent to which the model is substantially more accurate for some modalities (e.g., one ethnicity or gender) than for others within the same attribute, even when overall accuracy may appear high. sections.title: 5.3 Recall Divergence
sections.content: We additionally introduce Recall Divergence (RD), fairness metric designed to quantify disparities in per-modality recall within sensitive attribute. Unlike overall accuracy, which can mask systematic failures affecting particular modalities, RD focuses on how often the model correctly identifies instances when the true modality is given. sections.title: 6 Result
sections.content: 6.1 Instruction-tuned LLMs demonstrate zero-shot author profiling ability Fig. 2 shows that the LLMs are able to do author profiling only guided by prompt without specific fine-tuning. Indeed, all the models achieve better results than random guess for both the authors gender and ethnicity, with Mistral-24B achieving 76%2% and 44%2% accuracy respectively for gender and ethnicity with 95% confidence interval using stratified bootstrapping on songs. These results indicate that instruction-tuned LLMs possess non-trivial zero-shot author profiling capability. We provide the gender prediction distribution in Fig. 3 where we observe that DeepSeek-1.5B, Ministral-8B and Mistral-24B significantly over- (a) Asia (b) Europe (c) Oceania (d) South America Figure 6: Word clouds of the most frequently used words by DeepSeek-7B in its ethnicity classification rationales when being wrong. We highlight the words theme and emotional which we focused on in the paper. predict the woman modality. The ROC curve points present in Fig. 5 highlight the per modalitys predictions per model. More precisely, we see that the North American ethnicity has both high true positive rate (TPR) and high false positive rate (FPR) with Llama-8B, Qwen-7B, Ministral-8B and Mistral-24B. This pattern suggests that the North America ethnicity functions as default prediction category, consistent with the distributional shift observed in Fig. 4.

sections.title: Author Profiling
sections.content: Multiple factors have significant impact on the LLM ability to successfully achieve author profiling. We summarize here the results justified in the Appendix, Sec.D. First, The prompt used has an impact: using the Well-informed prompt, the LLMs obtain statistically worse performance results because of the prompts complexity. As seen in Fig. 2, using the Corrected informed prompt, the models achieve slightly better results for the ethnicity inferring. Across prompts, larger models consistently outperform smaller ones. For example, DeepSeek7B improves over DeepSeek-1.5B, and Mistral24B improves over Ministral-8B. Futhermore, we observe clear positive association between lyric length and profiling accuracy for both gender and ethnicity. We report substantial performance variation across musical genres, with stronger performance on rap than on electronic dance music for instance. We extensively analyze the translation impact and report overall translation-robust results.

sections.title: 6.3 Self-explanations
sections.content: We analyze the rationales of the LLMs when they are mistaken in order to understand what led to their errors. We thus consider the word frequencies of the self-reasonings. To isolate the false reasonings from regular explanations, we subtract the average word frequency to the false reasoning word frequency. Fig. 6 refers to word frequency for the ethnicity and shows that the model wrongly use emotional-based and themes-based arguments. Using this discovery, we created the Corrected informed prompt as defined in Sec. 4.2. This prompt enable less ethnicity-biased and more accurate predictions (next subsection and Fig. 2). This improvement supports the credibility of the rationales. Using the Well-informed prompt, we make the LLM evaluate 20 socio-linguistic criteria. One attribute evaluated by the LLMs is cultural references. We find overall highly negative correlation (0.31 0.02) between predicting the North American ethnicity and this attribute. This is more clearly the case for the American model Gemma12B (0.43 0.04) than the European model Mistral-24B (0.27 0.04). Our hypothesis is that the North American ethnicity, which we remind is the most predicted one by far, is the by default ethnicity, and that the models predict another ethnicity only if it finds clear indications justifying to do so. The ability of the model to find those indications is not given. Strikingly, with this prompt, some models failed to predict certain ethnic modalities entirely (e.g., Gemma-12B and Llama-8B respectively predicted no Oceania and South America instances, Qwen-7B predicted neither), indicating severe under-recognition despite balanced evaluation data. This shows, in our opinion, cultural blind spots of those models toward the concerning ethnic cultures. Note that the cultural references attribute has an important impact on the performance. It is positively correlated with the LLMs being correct for both gender (0.16 0.04) and ethnicity (0.28 0.02). We investigate in details other attributes in the Appendix, Sec. The abbreviations used are as follows: Sens. attr.: Sensitive attribute, DS: DeepSeek, SA: Sensitive variable, & : Informed and Expressive prompt, Corr: Corrected prompt, G.: Gender, E.: Ethnicity. 6.4 Bias evaluation We evaluate bias under two complementary definitions: distributional deviation from balanced ground truth, and divergence in per-modality performance. First, model would be considered biased if its predictions do not follow the groundtruth balanced distribution (Fig. 3, Fig. 4); we can evaluate this using statistical tests. Secondly, for each model, we compare the modalities performance with the average modality performance using the fairness metrics introduced in Sec. 5. Statistical tests The statistical tests indicate that Ministral-8B, Mistral-24B and Qwen-7B gender predictions are different from the balanced one: the three statistical tests (chi-2, CLT-based and Wasserstein-based) rejected the hypothesis that their predicted gender distribution was uniform. Concerning the ethnicity, for every model, at least one modality was over or under represented making the statistical test reject the hypothesis. For visual results and p-values, see Sec. in the Appendix. Fairness metrics Tab. 1 and Tab. 2 show that Ministral-8B is more ethnicity-biased than the other evaluated models. The least ethnicity-biased model is DeepSeek-1.5B, however this model achieve the worse performance, introducing the bias-performance trade-off. Gemma-12B followed by Llama-8B obtained good results with both the MAD and RD fairness metrics when evaluating the ethnicity. Concerning gender, the results obtained using the RD describe the opposite for DeepSeek1.5B which is the most gender-bias model. For the rest of the models, the results are consistent with the ethnicity-bias result: Gemma-12B demonstrates low gender-bias and Ministral-8B high gender-bias. Those results are coherent with the earlier intuitions of Fig. 3 and the the statistical tests results. Llama-8B obtained significantly better ethnicity-fairness score using the Corrected informed prompt than the Regular prompt, for both the MAD and RD metrics at 95% confidence.

sections.title: 7 Conclusion
sections.content: We study in this work the ability of instructiontuned large language models to perform zero-shot author profiling from song lyrics without taskspecific fine-tuning. Without fine-tuning, models achieve above-chance performance but exhibit sensitivity to prompt design, genre and lyric length. We introduce fairness-oriented metrics tailored to profiling tasks. Combined with other tools, they reveal systematic disparities across modalities, highlighting uneven cultural representation and potential blind spots. Analysis of model rationales suggests that explanation prompting can support interpretability, albeit with small performance tradeoff. Our findings indicate that LLMs encode rich stylistic knowledge but exhibit non-uniform behavior across cultural contexts, underscoring the need for careful evaluation of demographic inference tasks. Future work should explore author profiling in multilingual settings and mitigation strategies for culturally uneven performance.

sections.title: 8 Limitations
sections.content: Our study presents several limitations that should be considered when interpreting the results. First, the experiments are conducted exclusively on song lyrics, stylized and genre-dependent form of language; consequently, the findings may not generalize to other textual domains or communicative settings. Second, although we adopt the notion of ethnicity as culturally grounded construct following sociolinguistic literature, its operationalization through regional categories remains an approximation that simplifies complex and fluid social identities. Thirdly, when the goal is to evaluate the models ability to detect cultural identities, inferences based on an artist explicitly mentioning their own name in song are not informative. This occurred in small number of cases. Similarly but harder to remedy it, predicting the ethnicity from one specific localization-based reference is not the goal. Furthermore, We made the assumption that lyrics were written by the singer. This can be contested for two reasons: first, the existence of ghostwriters is well known; second, considerable amount of songs were written by multiple writers and not singular one. Overall, the songs in our dataset could have been better selected. Fourth, the use of translated lyrics further introduces potential stylistic distortions that may influence lexical and discourse cues despite our robustness analyses. While we have extensively investigated this point, it stays limit of our work. Finally, the diversity and training provenance of open-source LLMs remain partially opaque. When dataset origins and curation processes are undisclosed, interpreting cultural alignment becomes challenging. For instance, the observation that DeepSeek-1.5B aligns more strongly with Asian ethnicity predictions while DeepSeek-7B defaults more frequently to North American ethnicity may reflect differences in pretraining data composition rather than intrinsic modeling behavior. This lack of transparency limits causal interpretation of crossmodel comparisons.

sections.title: 9 Ethical considerations
sections.content: In this work, ethnicity is treated as sociocultural and analytical construct, rather than biological category. We use the term ethnicity to refer to similarities in broad cultural backgrounds; however, defining ethnicity through macro-regional origin is necessarily simplification. Cultural variation exists across continents, countries, cities, neighborhoods, and individual lived experiences, and our categorization should not be understood as exhaustive or definitive. Profiling sensitive attributes such as gender and ethnicity raises important ethical risks. Inferring sociodemographic characteristics from writing can inadvertently reinforce stereotypes, essentialize cultural expression, or encourage reductive interpretations of identity. Any observed correlations between linguistic patterns and demographic labels should be interpreted cautiously and must not be viewed as deterministic, predictive, or prescriptive. Our goal is not to classify or label real individuals but to analyze the behavior of LLMs under controlled experimental conditions and to examine how cultural signals are represented in model outputs. More broadly, our findings highlight the need for culturally aware evaluation practices, clearer reporting of model training data, and interdisciplinary collaboration between NLP researchers and social scientists when studying sociodemographic inference tasks.

sections.title: Appendix
sections.content: A Qualitative analysis: Examples of author profiling reasonings A.1 Featuring in songs: Eminem case study Box 1: Gemma-12B gender reasoning on Eminems Beautiful Pain The lyrics express vulnerability and emotional depth often associated with female perspectives in songwriting. Phrases like weep," feel so weak," and focus on forgiveness suggest personal and introspective journey, leaning towards female narrator. The emphasis on seeking light and healing also resonates with themes frequently explored by female artists. While not definitive, the overall tone and language choices point towards female voice. Although we excluded musical groups from our study, we did not explicitly remove songs containing featured artists, which introduced an additional source of noise. In several Eminem tracks evaluated with the informed and expressive prompt, most LLMs correctly inferred both gender and ethnicity. However, performance dropped on Beautiful Pain, collaboration with Sia, an Australian female singer. The presence of Sias verses appears to have interfered with the author-profiling task, leading some models to attribute the lyrics to female narrator. An example of Gemma-12Bs reasoning is shown in Box 1. Box 2: DeepSeek-7B ethnicity reasoning on Eminems Lucky You. The slang and urban references in the lyrics, such as GHETTO" and MARS," are characteristic of the African-American culture, which is predominantly from North America. Box 3: Llama-8B ethnicity reasoning on Eminems Lucky You. The reference to the Grammy" awards, which are awarded in the United States, suggests an American context. The use of phrases like bend the law" and cut the rules" also implies culture that values individualism and rebellion, which is common in North American hip-hop. The use of the word nigga" further supports this classification, as it is colloquialism commonly used in African American Vernacular English. Not all collaborations led to errors. For example, Lucky You, featuring Joyner Lucas (an American male rapper), was correctly classified by all models for both gender and ethnicity. Nevertheless, the explanations differed substantially. DeepSeek-7B relied on relatively shallow cues (Box 2), which could generalize poorly, whereas Llama-8B provided richer multi-factor justification (Box 3). This contrast highlights differences in reasoning depth despite similar final predictions. A.2 How can Miriam Makeba, Mama Afrika, be considered an Asian man? Miriam Makeba, also known as Mama Afrika, is renowned South African singer-songwriter. Her song Piece Of Ground4 was frequently misclassified by LLMs as being written by male artist. Box 4: DeepSeek-1.5B gender reasoning on Miriam Makebas Piece Of Ground The lyrics consistently use male pronouns (he," him") and refer to actions performed by the white man," suggesting male perspective narrating historical events and power dynamics. The language is also fairly straightforward and narrative, style often associated with male storytelling traditions. 4See full lyrics directly on Genius Box 5: Ministral-8B gender reasoning on Miriam Makebas Piece Of Ground The lyrics use the term white man" and black men" to describe the two groups involved in the historical conflict, which is typically associated with male perspective in this context. Box 6: DeepSeek-7B gender reasoning on Miriam Makebas Piece Of Ground The lyrics use terms like black man" and white man," common in blues and genres associated with African American artists, often written by female musicians. The narrative reflects historical struggles typical of female-centric stories in blues. Box 7: Mistral-24B gender reasoning on Miriam Makebas Piece Of Ground The use of me" and the phrasing of the observations suggest personal, reflective tone often associated with female perspectives. The lyrical style leans toward storytelling with emotional weight, which is more commonly found in female songwriting traditions. This example illustrates clear effect of model scale on reasoning quality. Smaller models such as DeepSeek-1.5B (Box 4) and Ministral-8B (Box 5) predicted male author, as did Llama-8B and Gemma-12B, often relying on surface lexical cues (e.g., references to white man"). In contrast, larger models such as DeepSeek-7B and Mistral-24B (Box 6 and Box 7) correctly identified female perspective, suggesting improved contextual reasoning with scale. Box 8: DeepSeek-1.5B ethnicity reasoning on Miriam Makebas Piece Of Ground The context of the discovery of gold and the transatlantic slave trade aligns with African American history, suggesting narrative from Asia. Although Miriam Makebas overall ethnicity prediction accuracy averages 59%, this particular song reveals several distinct reasoning failures. The most surprising case is DeepSeek-1.5B (Box 8), which predicts an Asian ethnicity. The model relies on partially correct historical references (e.g., gold discovery) but appears to misattribute the cultural perspective, illustrating how factual cues can be misinterpreted when broader geopolitical context is missing. Box 9: DeepSeek-7B ethnicity reasoning on Miriam Makebas Piece Of Ground The cultural references, historical context, and themes of struggle align with North American roots, particularly African American heritage. Box 10: Llama-8B ethnicity reasoning on Miriam Makebas Piece Of Ground The lyrics reference the arrival of white man" and the discovery of gold, which suggests European colonizers perspective. The mention of over the seas" also implies transatlantic crossing, common in North American colonization narratives. The reference to black men" and the historical date of 1883, which is significant date in the history of the American West, further support this classification. Both DeepSeek-7B and Llama-8B predicted North American ethnicity (Box 9 and Box 10), primarily relying on thematic shortcuts linked to colonization narratives. Our explainability analysis identified this behavior as recurrent source of error. Notably, when using the Corrected prompt, both models avoided this shortcut and correctly predicted an African ethnicity. In this case, the strongest explanations were produced by Mistral24B and Gemma-12B (Box 11 and Box 12), which grounded their predictions in historically specific references rather than high-level thematic cues. 14 Box 11: Mistral-24B ethnicity reasoning on Miriam Makebas Piece Of Ground. Box 13: Llama-8B ethnicity reasoning on Hayley Westenras Pokarekare Ana. The lyrics reference historical and social issues tied to colonialism, land dispossession, and racial segregation, which align with post-colonial African struggles. The mention of reserve territory" and the 30% land allocation strongly suggest South Africas apartheid-era policies. The gold rush context (1883) also points to South Africas Witwatersrand Gold Rush. Box 12: Gemma-12B ethnicity reasoning on Miriam Makebas Piece Of Ground. The central theme revolves around the displacement and exploitation of Black people by colonizers (the white man") for labor, specifically related to gold mining. The references to Black men," reserve territory," and the extremely low wage (six pennies day") strongly suggest colonial context in Africa. The phrase Gods own country" is often used to justify colonial claims, and the overall narrative of dispossession and marginalization is recurring theme in African history. The mention of gold mining further points towards regions in Southern Africa, like South Africa or Zimbabwe. A.3 References over socio-linguistics arguments: Cry me river Hayley Westenra. Hayley Westenra is New Zealand singer. Across her songs, LLMs struggled to identify Oceania as the correct ethnicity, predicting it in only 9% of cases. closer qualitative inspection reveals that models rely less on socio-linguistic writing style and more on explicit geographical references. While at most one model predicted Oceania for 14 out of her 15 songs, nearly all LLMs correctly identified Oceania (90%) for Pokarekare Ana, song containing an explicit regional reference. The mention of Waiapu" suggests connection to New Zealand, which is country located in Oceania. This geographical reference led me to predict that the writer is from Oceania. Box 14: DeepSeek-1.5B ethnicity reasoning on Hayley Westenras Pokarekare Ana which predicted North America. The lyrics do not provide any cultural or geopolitical references, indicating an origin more likely to be global or diverse, not tied to specific continent. The strong performance on this example appears to stem from the explicit mention of the Waiapu River, located in New Zealand, as illustrated in Box 13. The only model failing to predict Oceania was DeepSeek-1.5B, which instead predicted North America (Box 14). While its limited parameter count may explain the lack of geographic knowledge, the misclassification toward North America rather than Asia contrasts with patterns observed previously in our analysis, suggesting inconsistent fallback heuristics when explicit signals are absent. The Queen of Latin Music. Shakira is Colombian singer-songwriter, the LLMs obtained 65% accuracy on gender prediction and 43% on ethnicity classification on her songs. Box 15: Qwen-7B ethnicity reasoning on Shakiras Hay Amores. The lyrics reference the Magdalena River, which is located in Colombia, South America, but the overall poetic style and cultural context suggest European influence. The themes of love, nature, and time are expressed in way that is reminiscent of European literary traditions, particularly Spanish or Portuguese literature. Box 16: DeepSeek-7B ethnicity reasoning on Shakiras Hay Amores. The reference to the Magdalena River, located in Colombia, country in South America, and the cultural context of autumn themes often associated with Latin American traditions and seasons, suggest that the song originates from this continent. Box 17: Mistral-24B ethnicity reasoning on Shakiras Inevitable. The reference to parqués" is strong indicator of Latin American culture, particularly in Spanish-speaking regions. The phrasing and themesmelancholic, introspective, and emotionally rawalign with styles common in South American music (e.g., Argentine tango, Mexican bolero, or Colombian ballads). The poetic repetitionThe sky is tired of seeing the rain fall") is also reminiscent of Latin American lyrical traditions. The cultural habits mentioned (sleeping late, bathing routines) further suggest South American context. Box 18: Ministral-8B ethnicity reasoning on Shakiras Inevitable. The lyrics contain cultural references to European lifestyle, such as the mention of sleep and bathing habits, which are common in many European cultures. The use of you know me well" also suggests familiarity and intimacy that is often found in European cultures. In Hay Amores, the reference to Río Magdalena (Magdalena River) served as clear geographic cue, enabling several models to correctly infer South American origin, as illustrated by DeepSeek-7B in Box 16. However, Qwen-7B, despite recognizing the reference, prioritized stylistic and linguistic cues and predicted European ethnicity instead (Box 15). This divergence highlights how models may weigh cultural style over explicit geographical signals. In Inevitable, the mention of parqués, Colombian board game, provided culturally specific signal. Larger models such as Gemma-12B and Mistral-24B treated this reference as strong indi16 cator of Colombian or broader South American origin and correctly predicted the ethnicity (Box 17). Notably, models interpreted similar lifestyle cues (e.g., sleep and bathing habits) through different cultural lenses: Mistral-24B framed them within South American context, whereas Ministral-8B associated them with European culture (Box 18). Finally, Qwen-7B failed to recognize the cultural reference to parqués and instead inferred North American origin, as shown in Box 19. Box 19: Qwen-7B ethnicity reasoning on Shakiras Inevitable. The mention of football" (soccer), watch", and Sunday" indicates culture where these terms are commonly used, which aligns with North American English. The reference to cold" and breathing" could also be associated with colder climate typical in parts of North America. The overall tone and structure of the lyrics also reflect cultural pattern common in North American English-speaking regions. Linguistic attribute evaluation with the Well-informed prompt as hypothesis foundation B.1 Attribute evaluation The Well-informed prompt has two variants: the Attribute first, which evaluates socio-linguistic attributes prior to prediction, and Reasoning first, which begins with the author-profiling inference. We also evaluated the Well-informed prompts on DeepSeek-1.5B and DeepSeek-7B. However, these smaller models frequently produced outputs that did not contain valid author-profiling predictions, indicating difficulty following the complex multistep instructions being Chain-of-Thought models. As result, these runs were excluded from quantitative analysis. Similar issues were observed with Ministral-8B, which failed to produce valid outputs for approximately 5% of lyrics on the balanced dataset. Tab. 3 reports the mean attribute scores with 95% confidence intervals for each variant. Attributes are evaluated on scale from 1 to 10. Compared to general written text, the lyric corpus is consistently characterized by higher Emotions and lower Politeness and Formal language. sections.content: confidence interval Accuracy results with 95% confidence interval are provided in Tab. 6. We note that not all promptmodel combinations were evaluated. Preliminary pilot experiments compared the Regular and Informed prompts on Gemma-12B, Ministral-8B, and Qwen-7B, showing no statistically significant differences in accuracy. To allocate computational resources efficiently, we therefore restricted subsequent experiments to the Informed prompt family for the remaining models. DeepSeek-1.5B and DeepSeek-7B are excluded from this analysis for the Well-informed prompts, as discussed earlier: these models frequently produced outputs without valid author-profiling predictions under complex prompts, preventing reliable results.

sections.title: D Factors Influencing LLM Performance
sections.content: in Author Profiling We will in this section disentangle the role of (i) prompt design, (ii) model scale, and (iii) salient properties of the input text such as length, genre, translation and emotions in LLM-based author profiling. Prompt sensitivity and task recognition. Figure 8: Lyric length distribution given the gender. Figure 10: Lyric length impact on the gender and ethnicity accuracy using the ethnicity balanced dataset. Model scale. Across prompts, larger models consistently outperform smaller ones. For example, DeepSeek-7B improves over DeepSeek-1.5B, and Mistral-24B improves over Ministral-8B (see Fig. 2 and Tab. 6). This monotonicity is consistent with the hypothesis that author profiling benefits from higher-capacity representations that can better exploit weak stylistic signals in noisy, short-form text. Lyric length. Figure 7 shows clear positive association between lyric length and profiling accuracy for both gender and ethnicity. Performance increases monotonically with lyric length: longer songs expose more lexical, syntactic, and discourselevel evidence, increasing the available signal for inference. We omit in our analysis lyrics above 1000 words for two reasons. First, lyric length is correlated with potentially confounding factors, notably gender and ethnicity: for instance songs from North America are, on average, longer, which could partially account for apparent gains if not controlled for (see Fig. 8 and Fig. 9). Second and most importantly, the 95th percentile of lyric length is 964 words, so longer 21 Figure 11: Translation impact on the gender predictions per model using the gender balanced dataset. We show range value for the different prompt results. Figure 12: Translation impact on the ethnicity predictions per model using the ethnicity balanced dataset. We show range value for the different prompt results. texts would contribute limited statistical representativeness as illustrated by the density distributions mentionned above. We still provide the untruncated results in Fig. 10. Lyric emotions. We further investigate whether emotional content contributes to author profiling signals. To estimate lyric-level emotions, we use DistilRoBERTa-base model fine-tuned for emotion classification (Hartmann, 2022), which outputs probabilities over seven categories (anger, disgust, fear, joy, neutral, sadness, and surprise). Prior socio-linguistic studies suggest that emotional expression may correlate with demographic attributes, motivating this analysis. Using the resulting seven-dimensional emotion vectors as features, we train an XGBoost classifier (Chen and Guestrin, 2016) to predict gender and ethnicity. On balanced datasets, emotion-based predictions perform significantly above random chance, supporting the hypothesis that emotional signals contain demographic information. However, when training XGBoost on labels predicted by the LLMs rather than on ground truth annotations, the emotion vectors cease to be informative: performance does not exceed majority-class baseline on unbalanced data. This suggests that LLM predictions are not primarily driven by coarse emotional patterns alone, and instead rely on additional linguistic cues beyond sentiment or affective content. This result indicates that emotional cues alone cannot explain the biases observed in LLM-based profiling, supporting our earlier analysis that models rely on broader stylistic and cultural signals. Translation effects and regional English. Translation has limited impact on gender prediction  (Fig. 11)  , with translated lyrics exhibiting only marginal gains for some models. Translation impact on ethnicity however, shows more nuanced pattern in Fig. 12. At first, excluding Mistral-24B, we observe no significative difference between translated and not translated songs. However, Mistral-24B (the model used to translate the lyrics) have significantly better performance on the translated lyrics. It is noteworthy that Llama-8B and Gemma-12B accuracy in translated songs depend of the prompt type more than their non translated counterpart. North America being the most predicted ethnicity, we investigate using this modality in Fig. 13. English lyrics authored by North American (NA) artists achieve the highest accuracy, which elevates the aggregate performance of the untranslated condition. Strikingly, models often struggle with ethnicity prediction for English lyrics written by non-NA artists (second column), phenomenon particularly visible for Gemma-12B and Mistral-24B. We outline three plausible (non-mutually-exclusive) explanations: (i) non-NA English lyrics may contain more grammatical noise or non-native artifacts, weakening stable stylistic cues; (ii) non-NA English lyrics may be stylistically richer or more complex, increasing inference difficulty; (iii) models may be implicitly calibrated to NA varieties of English, making non-NA cues harder to detect, while translations may normalize text toward NA-like English and thereby reduce detectable regional markers. Disentangling these explanations would require controlled analyses that isolate translation effects from dialectal variation and language proficiency, for instance through matched-length subsets as songs from North American artists have in average more words in our dataset. While the impact of translation remains nontrivial, we conducted additional experiments to verify that translation artifacts were not the primary driver of our results. Specifically, we retranslated the original lyrics, restricted to the nonEnglish songs from the continent-balanced sub22 Figure 13: Translation impact on ethnicity prediction, highlighting the difference with and without North American artists using the ethnicity-balanced dataset. We show range value for the different prompt results. Figure 14: Musical genre impact on ethnicity and gender prediction using the ethnicity balanced dataset. set, using second model, GPT-OSS-20B (OpenAI, 2025). We then applied the Expressive and Informed author-profiling prompt to this alternative translation. Author-profiling performance was compared against results obtained from translations produced with Mistral Small 3.2 (24B Instruct). Excluding DeepSeek models, performance differences between the two translation pipelines averaged approximately 20% for the gender predictions and 25% for the ethnicity predictions. Among the evaluated systems, Gemma-12B, Ministral-8B and Mistral-24B exhibited the greatest robustness to translation variation. Translation effects were more difficult to characterize for DeepSeek models, for which prior work recommends aggregating multiple generations in classification settings. Llama-8B result on ethnicity varied on the non-translated text, and it is the only model which accuracy significantly changed from one translation to the other. Nevertheless, when considering all models, includ23 ing DeepSeek-7B, the overall conclusions of the paper remain stable, with global performance shifts limited to roughly 3% for ethnicity and 2% for gender classification. This global shifts performance is evaluated by averaging the absolute mean difference for every models. Musical genre as proxy for signal availability. Figure 14 demonstrates substantial variation across musical genres, with stronger performance on rap than on electronic dance music (EDM). While both genres are men-dominated in our dataset (women artists represent approximately 3% in rap and 14% in EDM), rap lyrics are substantially longer on average (623 words) than EDM lyrics (310 words). This aligns with the length effect in Fig. 7 and suggests that suggesting that part of the genre effect may be mediated by text length and information density rather than purely stylistic factors.

sections.title: E Statistical tests
sections.content: We show in Figure 15 the results of the different statistical tests evaluating the predicted distribution from an ethnicity-balanced dataset (or respectively gender-balanced for the gender evaluation). We observe whether the null hypothesis H0 "the empirical predicted distribution and the balanced distribution are equivalent" holds. If the statistical tests obtain p-value under 0.05, which represents the 95% confidence threshold, then we reject H0 and we conclude skewed ethnicity prediction distribution. We use three statistical tests, we therefore reject the null hypothesis if at least two of the three test reject it at 95% confidence. Concerning ethnicity, no model has uniform distribution for the Asian ethnicity, only DeepSeek1.5B do not reject the hypothesis for both Oceania and North America, Gemma-12B is the only model with possibly uniform distribution (test not rejected) for the South America modality. Concerning the African ethnicity, the hypothesis is not rejected with DeepSeek-1.5B and DeepSeek7B. Finally, for the European modality, only the Ministral-8B models predictions are statistically proven not to be uniform. Overall, no models prediction is uniform for the ethnicity, with the model DeepSeek.1-5B the closest one (two modalities have rejected uniform hypothesis) followed by Gemma-12B (three modalities have rejected uniform hypothesis). Those findings are similar to the one obtained with our fairness metrics. Only Gemma-12B gender prediction did not reFigure 15: P-values of the statistical tests per model and modality, evaluating the rejection of the hypothesis H0: predicted and balanced distribution are equivalent. The p-values are obtained through stratified bootstrapping. Models with p-values under 0.05 p-value threshold reject this hypothesis and are considered biased. ject the uniform hypothesis, making every other model statistically skewed toward either one of the two tested gender modalities. Non-Fairness Metric Evaluation As discussed in the main paper, standard fairness metrics are not directly applicable to the authorprofiling setting considered here. In our task, the target label corresponds to the sensitive attribute itself, which violates the common assumption that predictions should be independent from sensitive variables. Consequently, traditional group fairness definitions cannot be meaningfully applied. This limitation does not extend to standard performance metrics such as macro recall and macro F1-score. These metrics evaluate predictive performance by computing scores for each modality and averaging across classes. Although this procedure may appear superficially similar to the fairness metric introduced in our work, the underlying objectives are fundamentally different. Macro recall and macro F1-score measure predictive quality: for instance, random predictor would obtain low scores under these metrics. In contrast, our fairness metric evaluates bias rather than performance; therefore, random predictor may be considered minimally biased and receive favorable fairness score. To illustrate this distinction, Tab. 8 reports macro F1-score and macro recall alongside the fairness metrics previously presented in Tab. 1 and Tab. 2. Importantly, the interpretation of these metrics differs: for our fairness measure, values closer to 0 indicate better fairness, whereas for macro F1score and macro recall, higher values (closer to 1) indicate better performance. sections.title: G Comparison with other fairness metric
sections.content: on non-author profiling task. In this work, we introduce new fairness metrics because traditional fairness metrics typically evaluate statistical parity, i.e., independence between the model prediction and the sensitive attribute S. When the sensitive variable corresponds to the prediction target itself, statistical parity becomes fundamentally incompatible with predictive performance. This differs from the standard fairnessaccuracy trade-off usually studied in the literature. One of the most widely used fairness metrics is Disparate Impact (DI), which measures the difference in outcome probabilities between sensitive groups (of Justice et al., 1978; Feldman et al., 2015; Wright et al., 2024). For model ˆY = (X) and data distribution P, it is defined as DIratio(f, P) = min(P0, P1) max(P0, P1) or alternatively as the additive version . DIadd(f, P) = P1 P0. with, P0 := P( ˆY = 1 = 0), and P1 := P( ˆY = 1 = 1). The additive formulation is sometimes also referred to as disparate treatment. fairness notion more closely related to the type of disparity captured by our proposed metric 25 is Equality of Odds (EoO), defined as EoOratio(f, P) = min(P(0,1), P(1,1)) max(P(0,1), P(1,1)) . or EoOadd(f, P) = P(1,1) P(0,1) where P(0,1) := P( ˆY = 1 = 0, = 1), and P(1,1) := P( ˆY = 1 = 1, = 1). Equality of Odds evaluates statistical parity in the errors of the model. For both DI and EoO, the ratio definitions are bounded between 0 and 1, with values closer to 1 indicating greater fairness. In contrast, fairness improvement indicates lower fairness score with the additive definitions, with 0 corresponding to perfect parity. To better understand the interpretation of our proposed metric, we compare it with these classical fairness metrics on standard fairness benchmarks. Specifically, we evaluate the Recall Divergence (RD) metric in standard binary classification setting. For these experiments, we trained Random Forest classifier (BREIMAN, 2001; Pedregosa et al., 2011) on eight commonly used fairness datasets. First, we consider several datasets describing characteristics of the U.S. population in 2023, including income (INC), mobility (MOB), employment (EMP), travel time to work (TRA), and public coverage (PUC) (Ding et al., 2021). Second, we evaluate credit card approval decisions using the UCI Credit dataset (CREDIT) (Quinlan, 1987). (COMPAS) (Angwin et al., 2016). Finally, we consider the Law School Admissions dataset (BAR) (Wightman et al., 1998). The sensitive attribute varies across datasets. It corresponds to sex in the INC, BAR, COMPAS, and CREDIT datasets; to being older than 25 years in the MOB dataset; and to legal disability status in the TRA and PUC datasets. The fairness evaluations are reported in Table 9. In this experimental setting, the ground-truth label is not itself sensitive attribute. Consequently, our proposed metric, Recall Divergence (RD), does not measure fairness but rather the divergence in predictive performance between positive and negative classes. Its value therefore does not depend on the sensitive attribute S. Note that RD becomes + for the Travel (TRA) dataset because E( ˆY = 1) = 0, whereas this situation does not occur in the Mobility (MOB) dataset since E( ˆY = 0) = 0.000198. As expected, the interpretation of RD differs significantly from that of DI and EoO. For this reason, we recommend using RD as fairness metric primarily in author profiling tasks, where the target variable itself corresponds to sensitive attribute.

sections.title: H Dataset details
sections.content: H.1 Filtering As we aggregated songs from two sources, Spotify and Deezer, we had to remove near-duplicate entries such as reissues or slightly retitled versions of the same track. We therefore represented song titles using TF-IDF encoding. Within each artist, we compute cosine similarity between these TFIDF vectors and identify pairs of titles whose similarity exceeds 0.85. We then form connected components over these high-similarity links and retain only the earliest occurrence in each component, discarding the remaining duplicates. This yields deduplicated, consistently annotated set of songs suitable for downstream analysis of demographic prediction and bias. In our study, the result presented are from two datasets. The first, an ethnicity-balanced dataset. Creating such dataset was crucial to study ethnicity. Our original dataset was composed of majority of songs from North American singers ; with the models also predicting majority of North American ethnicity ; only with balanced dataset or recall-based approach would the problem been discovered. However, only using this dataset would have been an error. In fact, when analyzing the gender accuracy results with the ethnicity-balanced dataset, the Well-balanced prompt achieved the best results ; which is the exact opposite of the conclusion we have using the gender-balanced dataset. To analyse the gender error and biases, we had to use gender-balanced dataset ; futhermore, because we want to remove error from con26 founding effects as much as possible, we created the balanced-dataset from the ethnicity-balanced dataset. Because the gender was not naturally balanced in our gathered dataset, this induced significant loss of individuals for the gender analysis (from 3k singers to 2k). We argue that while keeping big enough sample size allowing statistically relevant results, we removed potential analysis error. H.2 Translation We translated all non-English lyrics to English prior to performing the author profiling task. This preprocessing step was necessary; indeed, certain languages, such as Spanish, French, and Italian, encode grammatical gender in verb conjugations, adjectives, and pronouns. For instance, first-person statement in Spanish explicitly marks the speakers gender through verb endings (e.g., estoy cansado" vs. estoy cansada", both translated to Im tired" in Spanish). Such grammatical gender markers could provide direct and non-linguistic cues to the speakers gender, thereby allowing the model to infer sociodemographic attributes from linguistic structure rather than from semantic or stylistic content. By translating all lyrics to English, which has minimal grammatical gender marking, we reduce this confounding factor and ensure that the models predictions are based primarily on thematic, lexical, and stylistic features rather than on languagespecific grammatical artifacts. The prompt used on Mistral Small 3.2 in zero-shot configuration is below. We languageidentification and filtering pipeline to decide which lyrics require translation. After normalization (removing invisible unicode control characters, unicode noramlization, removing non-linguistic symbols), we perform lyric-level language detection using langid and langdetect. To make the detection more stable on short lines, we split each lyric into sentence-like fragments and classify each fragment; we compute the proportion of fragments classified as English, and mark lyric as English if this ratio is at least 0.8. Lyrics below this threshold are flagged as requiring translation. In addition, we apply conservative sanity check" for lyrics initially classified as English: we tokenize the lyric and count tokens that are out-of-vocabulary (OOV) with respect to an English word list (NLTK words), and we flag as needing translation when lyrics contain more than 15% of OOV words. This implement automatic an second pass is intended to catch mixed-language or non-English lyrics that may be misclassified as English by the primary detector. Box 20: Translation Prompt Your task is to translate any non-English portions of the following song lyrics into English, while keeping any parts that are already in English unchanged. Instructions: - Check if the lyrics are entirely in language other than English. If so, translate the entire lyrics to English. - If the lyrics contain mix of English and non-English parts, translate only the nonEnglish parts to English. - Maintain the original structure, line breaks, and formatting of the lyrics. - Translate ONLY the non-English parts to English - Keep the original English parts as they are - Maintain the structure, line breaks, and formatting - If the entire lyrics are already in English, return them unchanged - Provide ONLY the translated lyrics in your response, without any additional commentary Lyrics to translate: lyrics Translated lyrics: All translations are produced locally with Mistral Small 3.2 (24B Instruct) served via vLLM on two NVIDIA A100 GPUs (80GB) using tensor parallelism. To ensure reproducibility, we use deterministic decoding (temperature=0.0) and fixed generation budget of up to 2048 completion tokens. Failed calls are retried up to three times. In our dataset, lyrics fit within the context window, so we did not require chunking. We use zero-shot translation instruction prompt designed to (i) translate fully non-English lyrics, (ii) translate only the non-English spans when lyrics are code-switched, and (iii) preserve line breaks and formatting while returning only the translated lyrics (no commentary). In practice, we observed that the model reliably detects nonEnglish spans in code-switched lyrics and translates them while leaving English spans unchanged. The exact prompt used for translation is referenced in Box 20. We manually reviewed translated outputs to ensure the model complied with the lyrics only" constraint (no meta-text), explanations, or analysis). Non-lexical vocalizations (e.g., la la la") were preserved rather than translated, and formatting (line breaks) was kept consistent with the input. H.3 Composition To construct the dataset, we initially collected song lyrics through uniform random sampling, without applying any selection criteria. An initial inspection revealed strong over-representation of songs originating from the United States. To mitigate this imbalance, subsequent data collection focused on underrepresented macro-regions. At no stage did we target specific artists: artists were neither intentionally included nor excluded during collection. Likewise, artist identity was not considered when constructing the balanced datasets from the original corpus. As result, some artists appear more frequently than others. In the ethnicitybalanced dataset, the most represented artists include Stef Bos, Lucky Dube, Akon, Nicholis Louw, and Miriam Makeba, each contributing at least 30 28 Model Prompt type Gender accuracy Ethnicity accuracy DeepSeek-1.5B Corrected DeepSeek-7B Gemma-12B Llama-8B Ministral-8B Mistral-24B Qwen-7B Informed and expressive Corrected Informed Informed and expressive Corrected Informed Informed and expressive Regular Well-informed Attribute First Well-informed Reasoning First Corrected Informed Informed and expressive Well-informed Attribute First Well-informed Reasoning First Corrected Informed Informed and expressive Regular Well-informed Attribute First Well-informed Reasoning First Corrected Informed Informed and expressive Well-informed Attribute First Well-informed Reasoning First Corrected Informed Informed and expressive Regular Well-informed Attribute First Well-informed Reasoning First 56 5 54 5 59 7 59 7 56 7 72 6 72 6 73 6 71 6 59 4 63 5 67 6 67 6 61 6 59 6 62 6 70 6 70 6 65 5 68 6 65 6 67 6 76 5 76 6 74 6 74 6 74 6 68 6 68 6 68 6 68 5 56 5 64 6 21 4 19 4 29 4 26 4 27 4 41 4 41 4 41 4 39 4 23 3 26 3 42 4 38 4 32 4 26 3 27 3 32 4 30 3 30 4 30 3 23 2 23 3 44 4 44 4 41 4 40 4 40 4 34 4 32 4 33 4 32 3 25 3 26 Table 11: Accuracy (%) using balanced dataset for the ethncity evaluation and the gender balanced dataset for the gender evaluation, for every model and every prompt with 95% confidence intervals obtained by bootstraps (1000 samples, 10% sample size per modality). songs. Confidence interval analysis: Stratified bootstrapping parameters The estimated confidence intervals depend on the sampling strategy employed. Empirical confidence intervals are computed based on two parameters: the number of samples (resampling iterations) and the sampling size (the proportion of instances drawn in each sample). In our experiments, all confidence intervals were computed using 1000 samples with sampling size of 50% drawn from the corresponding balanced dataset. This corresponds to 1000 resampled subsets containing 300 songs per ethnicity for the ethnicity analysis, and 1000 subsets containing 500 songs per gender for the gender analysis. To assess the sensitivity of our results to these parameters, we recompute the confidence intervals of model accuracy reported in Tab. 6 (Sec. C) using alternative sampling configurations. Tab. 10 presents results obtained with 100 samples instead of 1000, showing nearly identical confidence intervals compared to Tab. 6. In contrast, the sampling size has 29 Additional experiments showed that varying the temperature between 0 and 0.7 did not significantly affect author-profiling accuracy. larger impact. Tab. 11 reports confidence intervals computed with 10% sampling size (while keeping 1000 samples). Although the mean accuracy values remain similar to those in Tab. 6, the resulting confidence intervals are substantially wider. Under this configuration, the ethnicity accuracy of DeepSeek-1.5B would no longer be statistically above the random-guess threshold, neither for the Informed and expressive prompt (17 [15, 23]) nor for the Corrected prompt (17 [17, 25]).

sections.title: J Reproducibility
sections.content: J.1 Dataset Due to non-commercial licensing constraints, we are allowed to use song lyrics from Genius and Spotify but are not permitted to redistribute the lyrics themselves. To facilitate reproducibility, we provide the full list of song identifiers and metadata required to reconstruct the dataset from the original sources. Because lyrics were collected from two platforms, we include source column indicating the origin of each sample. Researchers can reproduce the dataset by retrieving the lyrics from the same public sources. J.2 Models All models used in this work are open-source. We provide the exact model names, checkpoints, and inference configurations in our code repository to ensure reproducibility of the experiments. J.3 Results The code used for inference and result analysis will be released on GitHub: https://github. com/ValentinLafargue/CulturalProbingLLM. We also provide the generated outputs to facilitate verification of the reported results on Hugging https://huggingface.co/datasets/ Face: ValentinLAFARGUE/AuthorProfilingResults. Reproducing the inference requires reconstructing the dataset from the original lyrics sources and running our scripts with the provided configurations. For the Informed and expressive and Welldefined prompts, generation was performed with temperature of 0.7, which introduces controlled stochasticity; therefore, small variations in generations may occur, although downstream evaluation metrics remain stable. For the remaining prompts, the temperature was set to 0, resulting in deterministic outputs under fixed random seeds and identical environments.

sections.title: L Author Profiling Prompts Used
sections.content: Author Profiling Prompt Regular You are classifier that analyzes song lyrics to predict two things: 1. The gender of the writer (male or female) 2. The continent of origin based on cultural references, language style, and themes (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, South America, Oceania) Return the result using this format: GENDER: <malefemale> CONTINENT: <AfricaAsiaEuropeNorth AmericaSouth AmericaOceania> Now classify the following lyrics: {lyrics}
